title: Paste from Buffer ({key:Ctrl-R})
subtitle: Same {key:Ctrl-R} as Insert mode — inject any register.
summary: From the command line, Ctrl-R{reg} inserts the register contents — exactly like in Insert mode. Lets you build commands from the unnamed register, search history, last yank, expression results, etc.

blocks.text: {key:Ctrl-R} on the command line works exactly like in Insert mode: it injects register contents into the line you're building.

blocks.title: Common Pattern: Substitute Last Search
blocks.text:
Searched for something with {key:/}? Now you want to substitute it. Two options:

  1. Use the empty pattern shortcut: `:%s//newtext/g` — Vim reuses the last search.
  2. Insert it explicitly: `:%s/<Ctrl-R>/` — Vim pastes the actual pattern, which you can edit before completing the command.

Option 2 is useful when you want to *modify* the search pattern slightly before substituting.

The {key:Ctrl-R}{key:=} expression register is also magical here — `:e <Ctrl-R>=expand('%:h')<CR>/foo` builds a path relative to the current file.
